prompt: Explain the water cycle, in prose, without using any lists, bullets, headings, or numbered steps. Keep it to one paragraph.
response: The water cycle, also known as the hydrologic cycle, is the continuous movement of water on, above, and below the surface of the Earth. It begins with the sun heating the waters of the oceans, lakes, and rivers, causing them to evaporate into the atmosphere as water vapor. This water vapor then rises into the air and cools, condensing into clouds. When the conditions are right, the clouds release the water they hold in the form of precipitation, such as rain or snow, which falls back to the Earth's surface. Some of this precipitation is absorbed by the soil and taken up by plants, while the rest flows into bodies of water or seeps underground to become groundwater. Eventually, this water is returned to the atmosphere through evaporation and transpiration from plants, continuing the cycle indefinitely. This process is vital to life on Earth, as it provides us with fresh water and helps regulate the climate.
